agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a significant change to a core logistics domain concept — the delivery promise — which directly affects customer expectations, SLA enforcement, and operational accountability.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking & Customer Communication","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the promised delivery window communicated to the customer, which sets the contractual expectation for fulfillment."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment made to the customer about when their package will arrive. Changing its granularity changes the nature of the promise itself."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is expected. Currently an exact timestamp implies a narrow window; date-only implies a full-day window (potentially 8-14 hours)."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual commitment to deliver within a stated timeframe. SLA measurement depends directly on ETA granularity."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"KPI measuring percentage of deliveries completed within the promised window. Widening the window mechanically inflates this metric without operational improvement."},{"term":"Last-Mile Delivery","definition":"The final leg of the delivery journey from distribution hub to customer address — the phase most affected by ETA precision."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The delivery promise communicated to the customer must accurately reflect the system's actual prediction capability. Over-precision creates false promises; under-precision degrades customer value.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Misalignment between communicated promise and actual capability corrupts the trust contract with customers and can create legal liability.","testable_assertion":"The displayed ETA granularity must be supportable by the system's prediction accuracy at a 90%+ confidence level."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Any change to ETA display format must preserve the ability to measure and enforce SLA compliance. The SLA clock must still have a well-defined start and end.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without measurable SLA boundaries, contractual obligations become unenforceable and operational accountability collapses.","testable_assertion":"Given a delivery with a date-only ETA of 'Friday', the system must define an unambiguous SLA cutoff time (e.g., 23:59 local time Friday) for compliance measurement."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Internal operational systems (dispatch, routing, driver assignment) must retain time-level precision regardless of customer-facing display changes.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch and routing require hour/minute precision to function. Removing internal time precision would cripple operations.","testable_assertion":"Internal dispatch records must continue to store and use exact timestamps even when the customer sees date-only windows."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Premium/express service tiers that promise time-specific delivery (e.g., 'by 10:00 AM') must not be degraded to date-only windows, as time precision IS the product being sold.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy tied to product tier definitions — a VP could redefine tier promises, but doing so would fundamentally change the product offering.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment with service tier 'express' or 'priority', the displayed ETA must retain time-level precision (hour or narrower window)."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Historical ETA data and reporting must not be retroactively reinterpreted under the new granularity. Past on-time rates must remain comparable.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Retroactive reinterpretation of metrics corrupts business intelligence and makes trend analysis meaningless.","testable_assertion":"Reports covering periods before the change must display metrics calculated with the original timestamp-based SLA, not the new date-based window."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A standard-tier delivery is in transit","when":"The customer views the delivery ETA","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no hour/minute precision"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An express or premium-tier delivery is in transit","when":"The customer views the delivery ETA","then":"The ETA retains time-level precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 10:00 AM Friday') consistent with the service tier promise"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery has a date-only ETA of a specific date","when":"The SLA compliance system evaluates whether the delivery was on time","then":"A well-defined cutoff time (e.g., end of business day or 23:59 local customer time) is applied consistently for SLA measurement"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The ETA display format has changed to date-only","when":"A dispatcher or internal operator views the same delivery","then":"The internal system continues to show exact timestamp ETAs for operational use"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The change is deployed","when":"Historical delivery performance reports are generated for periods before the change","then":"Metrics are calculated using the original timestamp-based SLA definitions, not the new date-only window"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Delivery ETA falls on a date boundary — e.g., ETA is 01:30 AM Saturday but customer sees 'by Friday' or 'by Saturday'","expected_behavior":"The system must resolve timezone-aware date assignment using the customer's local timezone, not UTC or warehouse timezone."},{"scenario":"Customer is tracking a delivery on the ETA date itself — they previously could plan around a specific hour, now they have no intra-day visibility","expected_behavior":"Consider showing progressive refinement: date-only when far out, narrowing to a time window (e.g., morning/afternoon) on the day of delivery."},{"scenario":"B2B customers with receiving dock schedules need hour-level precision to staff their dock","expected_behavior":"B2B/enterprise customers may require time-level ETAs regardless of display changes for consumer customers. Segment-specific display rules needed."},{"scenario":"Multiple deliveries to the same customer on the same date — previously distinguishable by time, now collapsed to the same 'by Friday' display","expected_behavior":"The system must still differentiate multiple same-day deliveries via order ID or other distinguishing information."},{"scenario":"On-time delivery rate appears to spike after the change because the measurement window widened from hours to a full day","expected_behavior":"Metrics must be reported with clear methodology notes; ideally dual-track both old and new measurement for a transition period to avoid misleading stakeholders."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Mixed. Short-term: reduced customer support costs if fewer 'where is my package?' calls result from removing a precise promise that was frequently missed. Long-term risk: degraded competitive position if competitors offer tighter ETAs. Premium tier impact unclear — if express customers are affected, revenue from premium services could decline. On-time delivery KPI inflation could mask real operational problems, leading to deferred investment in actual delivery improvement.","operational":"Positive for operations in isolation: wider delivery windows give drivers and dispatchers more flexibility, reducing failed deliveries and re-attempts. However, removing time precision from customer communication does NOT remove the operational need for it — dispatch, routing, and driver assignment still require exact times. Risk of organizational complacency: if the on-time metric improves mechanically, there is less pressure to invest in genuine prediction and routing improvements.","user":"Negative for most customer segments. Customers have come to expect time-level precision from modern logistics (Amazon, DoorDash, etc.). Moving from '14:30 Friday' to 'by Friday' is a significant step backward in customer experience. Customers who need to be home for delivery, or B2B customers scheduling dock staff, lose critical planning information. Customer satisfaction scores (CSAT/NPS) likely to decline unless the change is paired with progressive refinement (e.g., day-of narrowing).","risk":"High. (1) Contractual risk: enterprise/B2B contracts may explicitly require time-level ETAs — changing unilaterally could breach agreements. (2) Competitive risk: industry trend is toward MORE precision, not less. (3) Metric integrity risk: inflated on-time rates could mislead executives and investors. (4) Regulatory risk in some jurisdictions where delivery promises have consumer protection implications. (5) Customer churn risk if the change is perceived as a service degradation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Consumers (B2C)","impact":"Loss of delivery time precision reduces ability to plan around deliveries. Higher likelihood of missed deliveries if customer cannot be home during the now-wider window. Likely negative sentiment."},{"group":"Business Customers (B2B)","impact":"Potentially severe — dock scheduling, staffing, and inventory planning depend on time-precise ETAs. May violate contractual SLA terms."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Ambiguous — fewer complaints about missed exact-time promises, but potentially more 'when exactly will it arrive?' inquiries due to vagueness."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Minimal direct impact if internal systems retain time precision. Risk of reduced urgency if wider windows mask delays."},{"group":"Product / Business Intelligence","impact":"KPI comparability broken. On-time delivery rate becomes incomparable across the change boundary. Dual-tracking methodology needed."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"Enterprise customers may push back or renegotiate contracts. Sales team needs updated messaging about service capabilities."},{"group":"Executive Leadership / Investors","impact":"Risk of misleading performance metrics if on-time rate inflates mechanically. Must be briefed on methodology change."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal describes WHAT changes but provides no information about WHY this change is being made, which customer segments are affected, whether service tiers are impacted, what the SLA measurement approach will be, or whether B2B contracts have been reviewed. These are not ambiguous descriptions — they are entirely absent from the proposal. Without knowing the business motivation and scope constraints, the analysis relies heavily on general logistics domain knowledge and assumptions.","high_confidence_areas":["Internal systems must retain time-level precision regardless of display changes","On-time delivery KPI will be mechanically inflated by wider windows","B2B customers with dock schedules will be negatively impacted","Historical metric comparability will be broken"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether premium/express tiers are included in or excluded from this change","What the defined SLA cutoff time will be for date-only windows","Whether B2B contracts explicitly require time-level ETAs","Whether this change is motivated by prediction inaccuracy, cost reduction, or UX simplification","What customer communication strategy accompanies the rollout"],"assumptions":["The change applies to customer-facing display only, not internal operational systems","Premium/express service tiers should logically be excluded, but this is not stated","The system currently has time-level prediction capability that will be retained internally","The change applies to all geographies and customer segments unless otherwise scoped"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No business justification provided for the change. Is this driven by prediction inaccuracy (timestamps frequently wrong), cost reduction, UX simplification, or something else? The motivation fundamentally changes the analysis — if predictions are inaccurate, the change may be domain-correct; if predictions are accurate, it is a pure service degradation.","affected_stakeholders":["End Consumers","Business Customers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Proceed assuming prediction inaccuracy is the driver","Proceed assuming UX simplification is the driver","Request explicit business justification before analysis"],"recommendation":"Request the business justification from the engineering team before finalizing. The domain correctness of this change depends entirely on whether current timestamp ETAs are actually reliable."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information on whether premium/express service tiers are included in or excluded from this change. Time-precise delivery IS the product for premium tiers — including them would fundamentally alter the product offering.","affected_stakeholders":["End Consumers","Sales / Account Management","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Assume all tiers are affected","Assume only standard tier is affected","Request explicit tier scoping"],"recommendation":"Require explicit confirmation that premium/express tiers retain time-level precision before approving this change."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information on B2B contractual obligations. Enterprise customers may have SLAs that explicitly require time-level ETAs. Changing unilaterally could constitute breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["Business Customers","Sales / Account Management","Legal"],"options":["Review all B2B contracts for ETA precision requirements before proceeding","Exclude B2B customers from the change entirely","Proceed and handle contract issues reactively"],"recommendation":"Legal and Account Management must review B2B contracts for ETA precision requirements before this change is scoped."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No defined SLA cutoff time for date-only windows. 'Delivery by Friday' is not a measurable SLA without a defined cutoff (end of business? midnight? customer timezone?).","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Dispatch","Business Intelligence","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Define cutoff as end of business day (e.g., 18:00 local)","Define cutoff as end of calendar day (23:59 local)","Define cutoff as next business morning"],"recommendation":"Define an explicit, timezone-aware SLA cutoff time and document it before implementation begins. This is a prerequisite for any ETA display change."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this business domain change regarding delivery ETA display format.

This is a domain analysis task about changing ETA display from exact timestamps to date-only windows. Let me think through this carefully before producing output.

Classification: This is an ambiguity case. The proposal is vague - "date-only windows" could mean several things, and the business justification isn't stated. There are multiple ways to interpret this and critical details are missing about WHY this change is being made.

Wait - let me reconsider. The proposal itself is clear enough (change from timestamp to date-only), but the REASON and several critical business details were never provided by anyone. 

The spec distinguishes between ambiguity and missing context - ambiguity is when something is described vaguely, while missing context is when nobody provided the information at all. Here, the what is clear but the why, SLA implications, and contractual obligations were never mentioned at all. That's missing context, not ambiguity.
